### Post by SuSUntu on 2007-03-24
I know the tutorial you used doesn't say to do so, but modify your Section "Device" to look like this:

```

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"ATI Technologies, Inc. RV350 AP [Radeon 9600]"
	Driver		"ati"
	BusID		"PCI:1:0:0"
**	Option          "BusType" "PCI"**
        Option          "XAANoOffscreenPixmaps"
EndSection

```

Basically, just add the **bold** line shown above. That should do the trick.

---

### Post by SuSUntu on 2007-04-06
> **eauxnguyen said:**
> 

I tried your suggestion of adding the bolded line, and changed it to AGP, but it does'nt work. Do you have a suggestion?

Do you mean by the above that you tried both:

```

        Option      "BusType" "PCI" 

```

and 

```

        Option      "BusType" "AGP"

```

Or does it mean that you immediately changed "PCI" to "AGP" when you inserted it into xorg.conf?

If the latter, you defeat the whole purpose of that line of code. Basically, what it does is force "PCI" bus type in situations where the detected AGP bus type does not work properly. In other words, if you are using an AGP card and applied:

```

        Option      "BusType" "AGP"

```

you have explicitly applied an option that is implicitly applied by default, and therefore you have really done nothing.  

From the 'radeon' man page:

> 
Option "BusType" "string"
              Used  to replace previous ForcePCIMode option.  Should only be used when driver&#8217;s bus detection is
              incorrect or you want to force a AGP card to PCI mode. Should NEVER force a PCI card to AGP bus.
              PCI    -- PCI bus
              AGP    -- AGP bus
              PCIE   -- PCI Express (falls back to PCI at present)
              (used only when DRI is enabled)
              The default is auto detect.


Finally, if you did try 

```

       Option      "BusType" "PCI"

```

and it still didn't work, all I can say is that the problems of getting various video cards to do 3D is a pretty black art it seems. What may work for one person or a few people doesn't necessarily work for others. Even the BIOS version of your motherboard can have an impact depending on which graphics driver you are trying to use. It's a big mess, no doubt. Keep reading, though, when you've got the time  ... maybe you'll stumble upon someone who has found the one little thing that may make the difference for your situation.

Sorry I couldn't be of more help (and sorry it took so long to get back to this thread).


** BIG EDIT **

I just noticed you have 

```

        Driver      "fglrx"

```

instead of 

```

        Driver      "ati"

```

which implies you aren't using the Radeon driver or you have the driver option set wrong. I'm only personally aware that forcing the AGP-bus-to-PCI fix I posted works with the Radeon driver (maybe ... maybe not, I can't confirm),  but that could definitely be the most logical reason why my posted fix works for Sircoelho and myself (Radeon driver users) versus your situation.

---
